narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid family background in the high-income bracket and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several positive ways, including your LSAT score of 35, which is close to the typical student's score of around 37. However, the university's admission model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on past student data, estimated your chance of failing at 48.2%—high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your application. This prediction hinges on a few key aspects of your academic record that carried the most weight in the decision. The strongest factor pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, meaning your grades placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers that year, far below the average decile of about 5.5 and a clear red flag for bar exam success in the model's eyes. Next in influence, your LSAT score worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward passing since it was competitively close to what successful students typically score. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.8 also helped somewhat, nudging things positively as it wasn't drastically off the average of 3.2, though it wasn't a standout strength. Overall, your lower Year 3 performance outweighed these positives, signaling to the model a potential struggle on the high-stakes bar exam compared to applicants who maintain stronger consistent rankings. While your full-time status aligns with the vast majority of students who pass, it didn't shift the balance enough here. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your later-year deciles, perhaps aiming for targeted LSAT or GPA improvement through prep courses, and reapply once you've strengthened those core academic signals—the model responds well to upward trends like those seen in approved candidates.
